sentences.text: Yes.
Well, we came there in-- in '41 in the-- in the beginning of the year.
And we lived there for a-- for a certain time, until they told us that we had come to the ghetto.
Of course, I had to work there, too.
I was in-- in a place that was quite hard work.
It was outside.
And as a matter of fact, I got sick there.
I got pleurisy.
And-- but I-- I came back.
It was hard work.
It was in a-- in a-- they're cutting stones there.
It was quarry.
It was almost like a quarry, yes, cutting.
They needed some material for buildings.
He wasn't doing anything.
No, no.
He was going.
He could go.
Sometimes he got the permission, because there-- there was also a smaller Junderat there.
And they were-- they were doing there whatever they needed.
So they had to bring people.
When somebody needed, they had to-- they have to supply people for work if the-- if the-- if the Germans needed it.
My sister also, no, my sister was home.
She-- she didn't work at that time.
In the suburb we were, until they told us that we have to come to the ghetto.
So they started the ghetto in-- in end of '41.
And then they told us that we have to go to the ghettos.
Well, the ghetto consisted of a very small area.
There was two main streets in-- in-- in-- in the place.
And of course, in each apartment there were quite a few people.
The main street was Jozefinska and Limanowskiego.
This was the main-- these were the main streets.
When we-- when we came in, when we had to move into the ghetto, was the beginning of '42.
And the ghetto was created at the end of '41.
So at the beginning of '42, we came in, and we got an apartment at the edge street, on the edge of the ghetto, which was, the name was [?
Rinkafka. ?] And there was a house and we got-- one family lived there, but there were two rooms.
And we had one room, and they had another room.
And we started looking around.
My father did, when he needed to go out of the-- of the ghetto, he could get permission from time to time.
And I didn't work at that time at all, because I didn't have anything in the ghetto.
There was no real work in the ghetto, unless you work outside.
However, the-- the women could work already because there was a factory of uniform, which was outside of the ghetto.
But every day in the morning, they assembled in the ghetto and they went out there.
They called it Optima, because this was the name of the place.
Later on, this whole workshop was moved to the ghetto and it was administered there by-- by the people.
My sister and my wife worked there in Optima.
Well, we got married just before this, before entering in the ghetto.
My wife, it was-- her name was Irene.
And she was with her mother and with her brother.
And I knew-- knew them very well.
And later on, I worked with-- with her brother, and we worked almost at the same place all the time, wherever I took a job.
However, before I go further, I want to say that something terrible happened in the very beginning when we came into the ghetto, just after three or-- three months, maybe.
There was the first action on a large scale.
And this was the action that nobody knew.
Since it was the first one, there were no details known about it.
And they said that they are going to resettle the first people who don't work, or cannot have work in the ghetto.
They will be moved to the east for resettlement.
And-- and one day, it was surrendered-- it was surrounded.
The whole ghetto was surrounded by soldiers, so that nobody can go out.
And everybody had to come out of the house, and to go on a place.
In two places, they had it, and they checked everybody's identification.
And they decided whether he can stay or not.
I stayed because I got from-- from the workplace, not from the workplace, but from the people who handled this-- this workplace.
I got this that they can have for me a job, so I stayed.
My sister worked, so she went out.
But my parents didn't work, so they knew that they were not good.
When they assembled us all on the place, whoever didn't get the permission to stay had to go in-- in another-- in another line.
And they were loaded later on buses, and-- and from the buses on-- on the train and-- and further.
We did not know where the people went.
But this was the biggest knockout that I got during the whole war-- the first one, and the biggest one-- when I lost my parents at that time.
Because I-- I-- I felt inside that-- that although I didn't know where they're going, but they say to the east.
I understood that it will be a hard time that we will ever meet again.
And this became later known also.
What happened-- the first two days I was so knocked down, and so depressed, that I didn't even go out of the house.
I was staying in bed for two days, dressed.
I didn't undress.
And finally, after two days, I went out.
And pretty soon, we knew already that really, there was a tragedy, what happened.
And it was a real-- a real [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] what they called, and that the people some-- there was a boxer in Warsaw, a Jewish boxer, Rotholz, who went also with the same transport.
And he ran away in the-- on the way.
He jumped out of the car.
And then later on, he mixed with the Polish population, and he got the information what happened.
And he knew already, he told us that the transport went to-- to Belzec, and that nobody knew what was inside, because the crew of the train was changed from the regular crew.
They put a German crew inside, soldiers.
And then they knew-- they told him that after one day, the train came back empty.
And after a few days, a few weeks, nobody, nothing, was brought in.
Nobody was taken out from there.
Nobody knew what happened.
And we understood that probably they were all dead.
No, from Warsaw.
And he-- and he came into Warsaw.
He was here.
Apparently, he found himself in Krakow.
So that-- that's why he was there At that time, after this, I started looking for work.
And I got some work outside of the ghetto in a-- in a-- it's supposed to be a very good job because it was for the army.
And I got the permission to go out every day.

It was not far from the ghetto, but it was on the other side of the River of Vistula in Krakow, not far from where I-- where I lived before on the main street where the-- where the-- Of course, we had-- I had to walk, because I couldn't use the tramway, because they were not allowed.
Jews-- Jews were not allowed to-- to use it.
I started working there as a mechanic, or actually in the warehouse for parts.
I knew a little bit about it, so it was easy for me to do it.
The name of the firm was Steyr-Daimler.
And this was a big factory, the foremost German factory of cars and motorcycles.
And all these cars and motorcycles came back from-- for repairs to us.
I had there a-- which I thought that it was a friend of mine, who was a secretary of the university in-- in Krakow.
But as it appeared later, he was not such a friend.
So we were going from day to day there.
And my wife's brother also started working there.
No, we-- from the ghetto, we came out separately.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I-- I-- we received permission to go to-- to the-- to the-- for to go out from the ghetto, but at the same time-- the same time, they started building concentration camp Plaszow, not far away from the ghetto.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took us before, even before the liquidation of Krakow ghetto.
They told us that since we are going out into a German place, and the work was appreciated by them, they took us to the concentration camp about January.
I think January, 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My wife and my sister, everybody stayed here, but my brother-in-law went with me there.
Yes, it was outside the ghetto, but it was just nearby, the next street.
So I-- I started-- we started going from the ghetto to the-- to Steyr-Daimler.
And we went through the streets.
It was quite a long walk.
But we had always-- we couldn't go singularly.
We had to go in groups.
The whole group has to go together, because in the morning we had to be announced at the gate.
They counted how many we are.
And we had to come in the group, the same amount and to come back.
If somebody was missing, it was a terrible tragedy.
So we all went there, and there was an advantage that we could buy there whatever we wanted.
And we could bring it in for somebody else also.
In the beginning, of course, we didn't have to bring in, because we were alone there.
The families were not there.
But later, after the liquidation of ghetto, everybody was, of course, already in the-- Did they give you any food there during the day while you work?
No, they didn't give us, but we could buy there.
We had money at that time, you know?
They paid us.
There, they still paid us, yes.
And so then we had-- we were saved the-- the tragedy of liquidation of ghetto.
Because this was-- the ghetto was liquidated on the 13th of March, 1943.
And if you see [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the film, I think the advantage, the whole-- the whole value of this of the film, is that people can see it.

Because no matter of the account that I give, no matter-- no-- no matter what I-- what you interview me or what I will tell you, it still will not be the same that the future generation can see it on the film.
And people ask me sometimes, was it really so bad?
But it was.
It was worse.
I say that it was worse.
In some ways, it was worse.
Because there was-- there was shooting without limitation and brutality, which you cannot imagine.
If somebody couldn't go, couldn't work, he was knocked to the street with a bayonet, and killed right away.
If they wanted to kill any people in the hospital, in the hospital were killed on the spot, because they couldn't walk.

Certain nurses-- and there was for older people a geriatric hospital-- and the-- the nurses apparently gave poison to the-- to the-- to all the-- the old women and old men who were there, because they couldn't even walk out.
And wherever they came in after the liquidation, when they moved already everybody to-- to Plaszow-- not everybody.
They moved only to Plaszow whoever could work, or they see that he's young.
But if somebody couldn't, they shot on the-- on the space.
They-- they-- they shot a doctor at the hospital because he was doing an operation.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the street, certain soldiers, when they wanted to shoot, and there was a mother with a child, they told her to stay, so that he doesn't have to use more than one bullet.
They should stay in line.
And they went through from one house to the other one, and whoever was in the house, they shot there in place where it was, if-- if you didn't run away before.
This liquidation took one day, not a whole day even.
Of course, the Jewish policemen were also there.
They tried to help whatever the could, if somebody wanted to help, but it was not-- it was very difficult to do it.
No, they stayed there.
They stayed there, because they liquidated-- they still went through this whole thing for-- for-- for more than a week, to see whether nobody was hiding in the cellars or everything.
They couldn't do.
The first day they just do the rough job.
And the rest was-- the rest everything was done.
So this, we were saved.
And whatever-- whatever stories I would tell about the atrocities and everything-- [DOORBELL RINGING] Whatever atrocities-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
Whatever atrocities-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whatever atrocities they-- they were shown, I can give an account of it, will not be the same as what it really was.
It's impossible to describe.
There is in Polish a saying of a famous poet.
[SPEAKING POLISH]
Because this means in-- in translation--
In the translation language.
In a translation language, it means that the tongue doesn't-- the language doesn't express your-- your voice, and the voice is not an expression of your mind.
I think that-- that after, one of the-- of the writers of the Holocaust wrote-- wrote later on after the war, that no matter what we tell you, there is a line over which you cannot cross.

So whatever I will have to say about these things will just be, that's why this-- this-- I think that this is the value of the-- of the film, because if future generation will have to read it, it would be just something written, and-- and nothing will show up.
But from a visual point of view, the film will show really the atrocities which would happen-- which happened at that time.
Do you have an idea, more or less, how many thousands that they took from Krakow to Plaszow on that first liquidation date?
Any figures or anything, more or less?
They-- I-- I have to say something before.
After the first Belzec liquidation-- after the first Belzec liquidation, first transport to Belzec, the ghetto was made smaller because they sent out so many people.
So they cut the ghetto in half.
And we have to move, I with my sister, from the [?
Rinkafka ?] Street, we had to move into-- into a different place.
So we moved in one apartment, which had two rooms and a kitchen.
And we were a part of the kitchen.
We brought our furniture from there, our two cabinets, and we put them around, and we were in the kitchen.
And my wife and-- with her mother and her brother were in the last room, which was the room was also divided in half.
And another couple was in another half.
And the same was in the middle room.
No, everybody-- everybody had food for himself.
We got some food.
We received some food, and-- and-- in-- as long as we were in ghetto.
In camp, of course, in camp Plaszow, we had-- we had-- we had rations.
Yes.
There was no other waves.
There was only one wave.
This is all what came in the first day.
The rest, everything was sent out of Krakow, or-- or they were killed or they were sent out.
No, I don't know which camp.
They never said which camps.
Maybe later people will know, but we didn't know anything.
Now, in Plaszow, when they moved us to Plaszow before-- before the liquidation of the ghetto, there was one barrack only which was built, and they were still building it.
So we were in the first-- in the first barrack, which had space for it.
But they were building, and they-- they needed another two months for rebuilding.
And in the meantime, after, I know from now on, I can say that near Emalia, near the place of Schindler, there was already another factory there, the name was NKF, N-K-F.
That will be the letters.
And people were there also.
And they were also, before they-- they are going-- they were going out from ghetto.
And then later on, they were put, also prematurely, into the Plaszow concentration camp.
They made some electrical appliances for the-- there was something for the army, also.
That's why they went to Plaszow before, just like we went before.
No, they were separate.
Separate.
But they built it-- they built it quite, quite fast.
They were going up fast.
So when the ghetto was-- was liquidated, they had space for so many people.
I think 20,000 people.
Now, I continue to work at--
At Steyr-Daimler.
And we went from the ghetto, as I mentioned before, I was-- we were counted at the entrance and going out.
However, this was a small group.
We had to go out with-- with four soldiers, and we were walking on the street, not, of course, on the sidewalk.
And we-- we experienced anti-Semitic screams at us, and some people even threw stones, just from time to time.
The Polish population.
Despite the fact that we were-- that-- that we were the persecuted ones.

But the-- the good situation didn't take too long-- didn't last too long, because once they told us we were a small group, the other group from what I mentioned, the NKF, was going a little bit longer from that.
So they-- they asked us-- they asked us in the morning-- they told us on the gate that we will have-- that this group, our group, will only go out for the next-- till the end of next week.
And after this, we will have to come back.
We will have to look for another job.
So of course what could we do?
I started-- I asked my friend in the company, the Polish fellow, whom-- whom I gave some money.
I trusted some money, I gave him.
And I asked him to give me, bring me back, but nothing happened.
Nothing.
I didn't see one penny from this.
Apparently, they told me later that he-- he got drunk.
He made parties with the-- with the German soldier who was the contact with the army.
And I didn't get-- I didn't see any money anymore.
But on the Friday, we got whatever we could.
And everybody brought some-- some food, whatever they needed.
And they came, and we came back to the-- to the camp
This was the last day that we went out.
And we came to the-- to the gates, and one of the soldiers went to the-- to the gate, and he registered us that we are coming back.
But at the same time, the gate opened and a car came out.
And in the car was sitting the Commandant of the camp, Amon Goeth, with his chauffeur, with his Jewish chauffeur, who was on the side sitting there, probably tested the car.
And he jumped out of the car, and he came to us and he asked us what-- what are we doing here?
And then I told him-- because I was the head of the group-- I told him that we-- this is the last day that we are coming back from work because we couldn't go out.
We are a small group, and that we are waiting to get in.
The he asked us, and I was afraid of the question, what do we have in all these packages?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, I-- I told him that we have our uniforms here, and our tools that we have to bring back, because-- because we couldn't go out anymore.
We had to bring everything back.

And I was afraid that he will tell us to open the package, because there were already situations from others, that if they found something which you are not allowed to do-- and especially when it came to Goeth, who was a-- who was a terrible man.
As far as shooting is concerned, he could shot every-- could shoot everybody in the morning when they counted all the people.
He came out, and he shot somebody because he looked at him and somebody because he-- he looked aside from him, or whatever-- whatever his mood this day was.
But there was no question.
Sometimes people who came in, who smuggled something in, they hung and-- and he wanted everybody to be present at the same time when they hung him.
So I was afraid what his next request would be.
However, he noticed that one of my packages sticking out-- was sticking out.
And he asked me, what-- what do you have in this packet?
And I told him that I have a notebook.
And I wanted to reach in the packet, but then he jumped on me.
He caught my hand, and he told me, don't reach for the packet.
What is there in this book?
And I told him.
Finally, I took out the book.
He was reading it And what was in the book?
It was a book I had notes.
Whatever I have to do today, what I have to bring back, what I have to adjust, some people, some on the job, what I had to do.
He gave it back to me.
He hit me twice in the face, and he give me-- give me a kick.
And he said, OK, you can go in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we were lucky.
I think that this was a distraction, and that's why he didn't ask for anything else.
But it-- it reminds me a certain passage from from the film, where he-- where he, Schindler, tells him once, he said, a sign of power if-- if you can forgive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he stuck to it for a few hours, and then he came back to his normal life.
